**Synthesis of Governorâ€™s Language on Clean Energy and Economic Development**

---

### **Core Framing (March 12, 2025 â€“ State of the State Address, NC General Assembly)**

The Governor consistently connects *clean energy* and *economic development* as twin pillars of North Carolinaâ€™s prosperity. In the **2025 State of the State**, he situates clean energy within a broader narrative of competitiveness and inclusive growth:

- â€œ*Weâ€™ve become a leader in the clean energy economy*â€� (2025â€‘03â€‘12, State of the State).  
  This phrasing places clean energy squarely within the framework of business success and state pride, alongside rankings for business climate and job growth.

Economic development is presented as both achievement and ongoing project:
- â€œ*In recent years, weâ€™ve recruited businesses that have created tens of thousands of jobs across the state*â€� and â€œ*weâ€™ve announced more than 1500 new jobs, most of which are in rural North Carolina*â€� (2025â€‘03â€‘12, State of the State).

The speech ties sustainability to broad wellâ€‘beingâ€”linking environmental quality to affordability:
- â€œ*It also means clean air to breathe and clean water to drink. And it means lower costs*â€� (2025â€‘03â€‘12, State of the State).  
  Here, the Governor integrates clean energy and environmental protection with household economics, suggesting that these factors jointly define a â€œbrighter future.â€�

Workforce investments are described as essential to maintaining economic leadership:
- â€œ*Our goal is to set the national pace in growing the number of skilled workersâ€¦ Doubling the number of apprentices over the next four years*â€� (2025â€‘03â€‘12).  
  Clean energy leadership sits within this ecosystem of innovation, training, and â€œ*goodâ€‘paying jobs*.â€�

He also warns against complacencyâ€”â€œ*Other states want what we have here â€“ itâ€™s a competitive world*â€�â€”implying that continued investment, including in clean energy sectors, is necessary for sustained advantage.

---

### **Broader Economic Vision (Octoberâ€“November 2025 Columns)**

By late 2025, economic development messaging broadens beyond industrial recruitment to include resilience, innovation, and cultural vitality. In his **November 24, 2025 â€œ25 Things Iâ€™m Thankful Forâ€� column**, he credits:

- â€œ*The stateâ€™s economic development team for bringing more than 30,000 goodâ€‘paying jobs to North Carolina this year*â€� (2025â€‘11â€‘24).  
  This underscores the yearâ€‘long economic performance, though the clean energy theme is implicit rather than directly invoked.

Clean energyâ€™s early emphasis on leadership (â€œleader in the clean energy economyâ€�) evolves into a framing of *innovationâ€‘driven, diversified growth*:  
- â€œ*The culture of innovation that fuels our leadership in life sciences, aerospace, advanced manufacturingâ€¦*â€� (2025â€‘11â€‘24).  
  While clean energy is not named, its earlier positioning within â€œadvancedâ€� and â€œinnovativeâ€� sectors suggests continuity in the Governorâ€™s vision of a highâ€‘tech, sustainable economy.

The Governor also emphasizes sustainability through community and environmental stewardshipâ€”â€œ*park rangers and DNCR staffâ€¦ reminded the nation that western North Carolina is open for business*â€� (2025â€‘11â€‘24)â€”linking natural resources, tourism recovery, and economic vitality.

---

### **Evolution of Messaging**

| Period | Emphasis | Key Language |
|---------|-----------|--------------|
| **March 2025 (State of the State)** | Clean energy explicitly highlighted as part of North Carolinaâ€™s identity and competitiveness. Economic development expressed in job creation, workforce training, and rural inclusion. | â€œ*Weâ€™ve become a leader in the clean energy economy*â€�; â€œ*recruited businesses that have created tens of thousands of jobs*.â€� |
| **Octoberâ€“November 2025 (Governorâ€™s Columns)** | Clean energy folded into broader themes of innovation, resilience, and community economic strength. The tone shifts from achievement to appreciation and holistic wellâ€‘being. | â€œ*Leadership in life sciences, aerospace, advanced manufacturingâ€¦*â€�; â€œ*our diversityâ€¦ powers our economy*.â€� |

---

### **Summary**

Across 2025, the Governorâ€™s language on **clean energy and economic development** evolves from explicit recognition of North Carolinaâ€™s â€œ*leadership in the clean energy economy*â€� (March 12, 2025) toward a more integrated portrayal of innovationâ€‘driven, inclusive economic growth by late 2025. The persistent throughline is opportunityâ€”goodâ€‘paying jobs, workforce training, and broad geographic benefitâ€”anchored in sustainable practices that keep North Carolina â€œ*open for business*â€� and positioned as a national leader.

sources.title: state of the state â€” 2025-03-12
sources.chunkText:
Fundamentally, our commitment to our people is that they have a real shot at success â€“ to get a good-paying job or start a small business.

That's the promise of North Carolina â€“ that where you come from should never limit how far you can go.

Delivering on that promise is what drives me as your Governor.

In recent years, we've recruited businesses that have created tens of thousands of jobs across the state.

We've been ranked in the Top 2 states for business, four years running!

We've become a leader in the clean energy economy.

There is a lot to be proud of and plenty of credit to go around.

And I am committed to doing my part as Governor to continue our state's economic success.

In just the past two months, we've announced more than 1500 new jobs, most of which are in rural North Carolina.

But we cannot rest on our laurels. Other states want what we have here â€“ it's a competitive world.

sources.title: state of the state â€” 2025-03-12
sources.chunkText:
Emily inspired her sister Haylee, who is now pursuing her own nursing credential to work in labor and delivery.

Thanks to this apprenticeship program, Northern Regional Hospital is training its next generation of health care workers â€“ homegrown â€“ dramatically reducing its dependence on traveling nurses.

It's about turning apprenticeships into careers, which is good for both workers and employers.

Folks should not have to get a bachelor's degree to get a good-paying job and provide for their family.

Every hard-working North Carolinian deserves a real shot at a brighter future!

And we have to create the conditions that allow them to fulfill their potential.

Yes, that means good-paying jobs.

It also means clean air to breathe and clean water to drink.

And it means lower costs.

Too many North Carolinians are struggling to pay their bills, including housing and child care.

North Carolina is the third fastest growing state in the nation after Texas and Florida.

sources.title: november column 25 things im thankful for â€” 2025-11-24
sources.event: november column 25 things im thankful for
sources.chunkText:
4. Park rangers and DNCR staff, whose diligent work to reopen 13 parks reminded the nation that western North Carolina is open for business.

5. The tireless men and women at the Department of Transportation who have reopened 98% of Helene-impacted state roads to get people connected again.

6. The farmers who grow our food, shoring up our leadership in sweetpotatoes, pork, poultry, and more on the global stage, and the food banks that step up to keep people fed when other wells run dry.

7. The law enforcement officers who put themselves in harm's way to protect our communities.

8. Teachers who spend their days tying shoelaces, grading quizzes, and educating our next generation.

9. Our hardworking state employees â€“ the unseen heroes keeping our state running.

10. The state's economic development team for bringing more than 30,000 good-paying jobs to North Carolina this year.

sources.title: state of the state â€” 2025-03-12
sources.chunkText:
Each year, our state loses billions of dollars in economic activity because of our child care crisis.

So this week, I announced a bipartisan Task Force on Child Care and Early Education to find innovative solutions to this challenge.

When we do, it will be a win for our children, a win for our parents, and a win for our businesses and economy.

Let's work together to expand access to quality, affordable child care here in North Carolina!

To make North Carolina a state where folks thrive, we need to invest in children and their families by cutting taxes for middle-class families.

We can cut taxes to help families manage the cost of raising a child.

We can cut taxes to make child care more affordable.

And we can cut taxes on working families to put more money in their pockets, lift children out of poverty, and stimulate local economies.

These three targeted tax cuts are what being pro-family looks like!

sources.title: november column 25 things im thankful for â€” 2025-11-24
sources.event: november column 25 things im thankful for
sources.chunkText:
11. People in Southport, Charlotte, Maxton, and across the state who have comforted their neighbors after violence.

12. North Carolina's diversity, which powers our economy and enriches our lives.

13. The culture of innovation that fuels our leadership in life sciences, aerospace, advanced manufacturing, and even dinosaur discoveries.

14. North Carolina barbecue, tacos, and seafood.

15. Our 58 community colleges that train people for the jobs of today and tomorrow, and our top-tier research universities that push the boundaries of innovation.

16. The chance to listen to North Carolina's incredible musical talent at Dreamville and Merlefest.

17. The thriving statewide craft beer industry â€“ and the more than $2 billion in annual economic impact it creates.

18. Working with NCDHHS and Undue Medical Debt to relieve $6.5 billion in medical debt for more than 2.5 million North Carolinians.

sources.title: state of the state â€” 2025-03-12
sources.chunkText:
We can afford them and still meet our critical education, health care, and public safety needs.

But we won't be able to make these necessary investments if we do not address the self-inflicted fiscal cliff we face in a couple of years, especially given today's uncertainties.

We're at a fork in the road when it comes to our budget.

We can go ahead with nearly $10 billion of tax giveaways over the next 4 years, mainly to the wealthy and to corporate shareholders.

Or we can help our neighbors recover from Hurricane Helene, invest in our public safety and public schools, provide targeted tax cuts to support our working families, and keep up with the needs of our fast-growing population.

So what's it going to be? Give money to out of state shareholders, or invest in North Carolina families?

We cannot afford to do both.

We should choose North Carolina's children and families every time!

Of course, we have to be fiscally prudent â€“ to be smart about how we invest taxpayers' dollars.

sources.title: state of the state â€” 2025-03-12
sources.chunkText:
We cannot afford to leave anyone's talents on the sidelines â€“ we must be a state that is welcoming to all people.

We must continue to grow our economy and ensure it works for everyone!

The people of North Carolina are our greatest asset.

So to invest in our future, we must invest in our people.

And know this: while I am governor, when it comes to developing our workforce, no state will outwork us!

Our goal is to set the national pace in growing the number of skilled workers, including:

Doubling the number of apprentices over the next four years, and

Empowering hundreds of thousands more North Carolinians with the credentials they need to secure a good-paying job.

So, I'm proposing that for students getting certified in high-demand sectors, like in advanced manufacturing, health care, and IT, we provide them free community college!

We've also got to support Propel NC, which rewards community colleges when students earn credentials for high wage jobs.

sources.title: october column a healthier nc means a safer nc â€” 2025-10-27
sources.event: october column a healthier nc means a safer nc
sources.chunkText:
Finally, we must do more to ensure that people who reenter society after incarceration are ready to lead productive, law-abiding lives that contribute to their communities. That means supporting them as they search for housing, health care, and job opportunities. The reality is that 95% of people in prison will eventually return to cities and towns across North Carolina. If we close doors of opportunity for them, it's that much more likely that they'll return to the same choices that put them in jail in the first place. Let's help people succeed with their second chance.

The Time Is Now

The connection between public safety and mental health is undeniable. If we invest in one, then we strengthen the other. North Carolinians deserve a government that protects their safety and supports their well-being. The legislature has the chance to do both by passing the public safety package, finally fully funding Medicaid, and making smart investments in mental health.

Let's get it done. Together, let's build a safer, stronger, and healthier North Carolina.

sources.title: state of the state â€” 2025-03-12
sources.chunkText:
The crews and contractors of the Department of Transportation have reopened more than 1,300 roads so far.

And as of March First, I-40 is open! That's great work, Secretary Hopkins.

We've removed nearly 5 million cubic yards of debris from our roadways.

Half of all state parks and cultural sites hit by the storm have fully reopened, and almost all of them are accepting visitors.

So many people and organizations, public and private, have worked so hard.

I saw firsthand the remarkable efforts of Baptists on Mission in mid-January when I volunteered with them to repair the home of a schoolteacher in Spruce Pine.

Groups like Baptists on Mission and Habitat for Humanity are working tirelessly to get people back into their homes.

We need them to keep up their good work.

That's why, in January, I announced $6 million in grants to these two organizations!

In addition to addressing housing, we've been focused on supporting struggling small businesses.

sources.title: state of the state â€” 2025-03-12
sources.chunkText:
But our housing supply has not kept up.

To keep prices down, we need more housing of all types â€“ single family, duplexes, triplexes, condos, apartments, special needs housing.

We can find bi-partisan solutions to high housing costs if we're willing to work together.

So let's do it and get more homes built for our people!

It's not just housing that's in short supply; we also need more child care options.

You want to hear something crazy â€“ too many parents cannot afford to work.

That's because they can't find child care they can afford, if they can find it all.

At the same time, we don't have enough child care workers because they don't get paid enough.

Currently, there is only 1 child care spot for every 5 families wanting one.

Of course, parents should always have the option to stay home with their children.

But it should be a choice â€“ parents shouldn't be pushed out of the workforce.

When they are, they suffer â€“ and our economy suffers.
